question:
Let $A$ be the set of all integers $n$ such that $1 \leq n \leq 2021$ and $\gcd(n, 2021) = 1$.
For every nonnegative integer $j$, let
\[
S(j) = \sum_{n \in A} n^j.
\]
Determine all values of $j$ such that $S(j)$ is a multiple of 2021.
solution:
The values of $j$ in question are those not divisible by either $42$ or $46$.

We first check that for $p$ prime,
\[
\sum_{n=1}^{p-1} n^j \equiv 0 \pmod{p} \Leftrightarrow j \not\equiv 0 \pmod{p-1}.
\]
If $j \equiv 0 \pmod{p-1}$, then $n^j \equiv 1 \pmod{p}$ for each $n$, so $\sum_{n=1}^{p-1} n^j \equiv p-1 \pmod{p}$. If $j \not\equiv 0 \pmod{p-1}$, we can pick a primitive root $m$ modulo $p$,
observe that $m^j \not\equiv 1 \pmod{p}$, and then note that
\[
\sum_{n=1}^{p-1} n^j \equiv \sum_{n=1}^{p-1} (mn)^j = m^j \sum_{n=1}^{p-1} n^j \pmod{p},
\]
which is only possible if $\sum_{n=1}^{p-1} n^j \equiv 0 \pmod{p}$.

We now note that the prime factorization of 2021 is $43 \times 47$,
so it suffices to determine when $S(j)$ is divisible by each of 43 and 47.
We have
\begin{align*}
S(j) &\equiv 46 \sum_{n=1}^{42} n^j \pmod{43} \\
S(j) &\equiv 42 \sum_{n=1}^{46} n^j \pmod{47}.
\end{align*}
Since 46 and 42 are coprime to 43 and 47, respectively, 
we have 
\begin{gather*}
S(j) \equiv 0 \pmod{43} \Leftrightarrow j \not\equiv 0 \pmod{42} \\
S(j) \equiv 0 \pmod{47} \Leftrightarrow j \not\equiv 0 \pmod{46}.
\end{gather*}
This yields the claimed result.

descriptive_long.question:
Let $\coprimeset$ be the set of all integers $\integern$ such that $1 \leq \integern \leq 2021$ and $\gcd(\integern, 2021) = 1$. For every nonnegative integer $\exponentj$, let
\[
\sumpowers(\exponentj) = \sum_{\integern \in \coprimeset} \integern^{\exponentj}.
\]
Determine all values of $\exponentj$ such that $\sumpowers(\exponentj)$ is a multiple of 2021.
descriptive_long.solution:
The values of $\exponentj$ in question are those not divisible by either $42$ or $46$.

We first check that for $\primebase$ prime,
\[
\sum_{\integern=1}^{\primebase-1} \integern^{\exponentj} \equiv 0 \pmod{\primebase} \Leftrightarrow \exponentj \not\equiv 0 \pmod{\primebase-1}.
\]
If $\exponentj \equiv 0 \pmod{\primebase-1}$, then $\integern^{\exponentj} \equiv 1 \pmod{\primebase}$ for each $\integern$, so $\sum_{\integern=1}^{\primebase-1} \integern^{\exponentj} \equiv \primebase-1 \pmod{\primebase}$. If $\exponentj \not\equiv 0 \pmod{\primebase-1}$, we can pick a primitive root $\primrootm$ modulo $\primebase$, observe that $\primrootm^{\exponentj} \not\equiv 1 \pmod{\primebase}$, and then note that
\[
\sum_{\integern=1}^{\primebase-1} \integern^{\exponentj} \equiv \sum_{\integern=1}^{\primebase-1} (\primrootm\integern)^{\exponentj} = \primrootm^{\exponentj} \sum_{\integern=1}^{\primebase-1} \integern^{\exponentj} \pmod{\primebase},
\]
which is only possible if $\sum_{\integern=1}^{\primebase-1} \integern^{\exponentj} \equiv 0 \pmod{\primebase}$.

We now note that the prime factorization of 2021 is $43 \times 47$, so it suffices to determine when $\sumpowers(\exponentj)$ is divisible by each of 43 and 47. We have
\begin{align*}
\sumpowers(\exponentj) &\equiv 46 \sum_{\integern=1}^{42} \integern^{\exponentj} \pmod{43} \\
\sumpowers(\exponentj) &\equiv 42 \sum_{\integern=1}^{46} \integern^{\exponentj} \pmod{47}.
\end{align*}
Since 46 and 42 are coprime to 43 and 47, respectively,
we have
\begin{gather*}
\sumpowers(\exponentj) \equiv 0 \pmod{43} \Leftrightarrow \exponentj \not\equiv 0 \pmod{42} \\
\sumpowers(\exponentj) \equiv 0 \pmod{47} \Leftrightarrow \exponentj \not\equiv 0 \pmod{46}.
\end{gather*}
This yields the claimed result.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Let $compassrose$ be the set of all integers $pinecone$ such that $1 \leq pinecone \leq 2021$ and $\gcd(pinecone, 2021) = 1$.
For every nonnegative integer $lighthouse$, let
\[
harmonica(lighthouse) = \sum_{pinecone \in compassrose} pinecone^{lighthouse}.
\]
Determine all values of $lighthouse$ such that $harmonica(lighthouse)$ is a multiple of 2021.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
The values of $lighthouse$ in question are those not divisible by either $42$ or $46$.

We first check that for $fireplace$ prime,
\[
\sum_{pinecone=1}^{fireplace-1} pinecone^{lighthouse} \equiv 0 \pmod{fireplace} \Leftrightarrow lighthouse \not\equiv 0 \pmod{fireplace-1}.
\]
If $lighthouse \equiv 0 \pmod{fireplace-1}$, then $pinecone^{lighthouse} \equiv 1 \pmod{fireplace}$ for each $pinecone$, so $\sum_{pinecone=1}^{fireplace-1} pinecone^{lighthouse} \equiv fireplace-1 \pmod{fireplace}$. If $lighthouse \not\equiv 0 \pmod{fireplace-1}$, we can pick a primitive root $butterfly$ modulo $fireplace$,
observe that $butterfly^{lighthouse} \not\equiv 1 \pmod{fireplace}$, and then note that
\[
\sum_{pinecone=1}^{fireplace-1} pinecone^{lighthouse} \equiv \sum_{pinecone=1}^{fireplace-1} (butterfly\,pinecone)^{lighthouse} = butterfly^{lighthouse} \sum_{pinecone=1}^{fireplace-1} pinecone^{lighthouse} \pmod{fireplace},
\]
which is only possible if $\sum_{pinecone=1}^{fireplace-1} pinecone^{lighthouse} \equiv 0 \pmod{fireplace}$.

We now note that the prime factorization of 2021 is $43 \times 47$,
so it suffices to determine when $harmonica(lighthouse)$ is divisible by each of 43 and 47.
We have
\begin{align*}
harmonica(lighthouse) &\equiv 46 \sum_{pinecone=1}^{42} pinecone^{lighthouse} \pmod{43} \\
harmonica(lighthouse) &\equiv 42 \sum_{pinecone=1}^{46} pinecone^{lighthouse} \pmod{47}.
\end{align*}
Since 46 and 42 are coprime to 43 and 47, respectively, 
we have 
\begin{gather*}
harmonica(lighthouse) \equiv 0 \pmod{43} \Leftrightarrow lighthouse \not\equiv 0 \pmod{42} \\
harmonica(lighthouse) \equiv 0 \pmod{47} \Leftrightarrow lighthouse \not\equiv 0 \pmod{46}.
\end{gather*}
This yields the claimed result.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Let $noncoprime$ be the set of all integers $\fractional$ such that $1 \leq \fractional \leq 2021$ and $\gcd(\fractional, 2021) = 1$. For every nonnegative integer $\denominator$, let
\[
\difference(\denominator) = \sum_{\fractional \in noncoprime} \fractional^{\denominator}.
\]
Determine all values of $\denominator$ such that $\difference(\denominator)$ is a multiple of 2021.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
The values of $\denominator$ in question are those not divisible by either 42 or 46.

We first check that for $\nonprime$ prime,
\[
\sum_{\fractional=1}^{\nonprime-1} \fractional^{\denominator} \equiv 0 \pmod{\nonprime} \Leftrightarrow \denominator \not\equiv 0 \pmod{\nonprime-1}.
\]
If $\denominator \equiv 0 \pmod{\nonprime-1}$, then $\fractional^{\denominator} \equiv 1 \pmod{\nonprime}$ for each $\fractional$, so $\sum_{\fractional=1}^{\nonprime-1} \fractional^{\denominator} \equiv \nonprime-1 \pmod{\nonprime}$. If $\denominator \not\equiv 0 \pmod{\nonprime-1}$, we can pick a primitive root $\composite$ modulo $\nonprime$, observe that $\composite^{\denominator} \not\equiv 1 \pmod{\nonprime}$, and then note that
\[
\sum_{\fractional=1}^{\nonprime-1} (\composite\,\fractional)^{\denominator} = \composite^{\denominator} \sum_{\fractional=1}^{\nonprime-1} \fractional^{\denominator} \pmod{\nonprime},
\]
which is only possible if $\sum_{\fractional=1}^{\nonprime-1} \fractional^{\denominator} \equiv 0 \pmod{\nonprime}$.

We now note that the prime factorization of 2021 is $43 \times 47$, so it suffices to determine when $\difference(\denominator)$ is divisible by each of 43 and 47. We have
\begin{align*}
\difference(\denominator) &\equiv 46 \sum_{\fractional=1}^{42} \fractional^{\denominator} \pmod{43} \\
\difference(\denominator) &\equiv 42 \sum_{\fractional=1}^{46} \fractional^{\denominator} \pmod{47}.
\end{align*}
Since 46 and 42 are coprime to 43 and 47, respectively,
\begin{gather*}
\difference(\denominator) \equiv 0 \pmod{43} \Leftrightarrow \denominator \not\equiv 0 \pmod{42} \\
\difference(\denominator) \equiv 0 \pmod{47} \Leftrightarrow \denominator \not\equiv 0 \pmod{46}.
\end{gather*}
This yields the claimed result.

garbled_string.question:
Let $asdfghjk$ be the set of all integers $qzxwvtnp$ such that $1 \leq qzxwvtnp \leq 2021$ and $\gcd(qzxwvtnp, 2021) = 1$.
For every nonnegative integer $hjgrksla$, let
\[
poiuytre(hjgrksla) = \sum_{qzxwvtnp \in asdfghjk} qzxwvtnp^{hjgrksla}.
\]
Determine all values of $hjgrksla$ such that $poiuytre(hjgrksla)$ is a multiple of 2021.
garbled_string.solution:
The values of $hjgrksla$ in question are those not divisible by either $42$ or $46$.

We first check that for $zxccvbnm$ prime,
\[
\sum_{qzxwvtnp=1}^{zxccvbnm-1} qzxwvtnp^{hjgrksla} \equiv 0 \pmod{zxccvbnm} \Leftrightarrow hjgrksla 
ot\equiv 0 \pmod{zxccvbnm-1}.
\]
If $hjgrksla \equiv 0 \pmod{zxccvbnm-1}$, then $qzxwvtnp^{hjgrksla} \equiv 1 \pmod{zxccvbnm}$ for each $qzxwvtnp$, so $\sum_{qzxwvtnp=1}^{zxccvbnm-1} qzxwvtnp^{hjgrksla} \equiv zxccvbnm-1 \pmod{zxccvbnm}$. If $hjgrksla 
ot\equiv 0 \pmod{zxccvbnm-1}$, we can pick a primitive root $lkjhgfas$ modulo $zxccvbnm$,
observe that $lkjhgfas^{hjgrksla} 
ot\equiv 1 \pmod{zxccvbnm}$, and then note that
\[
\sum_{qzxwvtnp=1}^{zxccvbnm-1} qzxwvtnp^{hjgrksla} \equiv \sum_{qzxwvtnp=1}^{zxccvbnm-1} (lkjhgfas qzxwvtnp)^{hjgrksla} = lkjhgfas^{hjgrksla} \sum_{qzxwvtnp=1}^{zxccvbnm-1} qzxwvtnp^{hjgrksla} \pmod{zxccvbnm},
\]
which is only possible if $\sum_{qzxwvtnp=1}^{zxccvbnm-1} qzxwvtnp^{hjgrksla} \equiv 0 \pmod{zxccvbnm}$.

We now note that the prime factorization of 2021 is $43 	imes 47$,
so it suffices to determine when $poiuytre(hjgrksla)$ is divisible by each of 43 and 47.
We have
 egin{align*}
poiuytre(hjgrksla) &\equiv 46 \sum_{qzxwvtnp=1}^{42} qzxwvtnp^{hjgrksla} \pmod{43} \npoiuytre(hjgrksla) &\equiv 42 \sum_{qzxwvtnp=1}^{46} qzxwvtnp^{hjgrksla} \pmod{47}.
\end{align*}
Since 46 and 42 are coprime to 43 and 47, respectively, 
we have 
 egin{gather*}
poiuytre(hjgrksla) \equiv 0 \pmod{43} \Leftrightarrow hjgrksla 
ot\equiv 0 \pmod{42} \npoiuytre(hjgrksla) \equiv 0 \pmod{47} \Leftrightarrow hjgrksla 
ot\equiv 0 \pmod{46}.
\end{gather*}
This yields the claimed result.

kernel_variant.question:
Let  
\[
N \;=\;2^{5}\times 43^{2}\times 47^{2}=130\,702\,112 ,\qquad 
A \;=\;\Bigl\{\,n\in\mathbf Z \;:\; 1\le n\le N,\;
                    \gcd (n,N)=1\Bigr\}.
\]

For every integer $j\ge 0$ put  
\[
S(j)\;=\;\sum_{\,n\in A} n^{\,j}\quad\bigl(j\in\mathbf Z_{\ge 0}\bigr),
\qquad 
\nu _{p}(m)=\max\bigl\{\,e\ge0 : p^{e}\mid m\bigr\}
\;(p\;\hbox{ prime}).
\]

(The symbol $\nu _{p}$ denotes the usual $p$-adic valuation.)

1.  Determine $\nu_{2}\!\bigl(S(j)\bigr)$ for every $j\ge 0$.

2.  Determine $\nu_{43}\!\bigl(S(j)\bigr)$ and decide \emph{precisely}
    for which exponents $j$ one has $43^{3}\mid S(j)$.

3.  Determine $\nu_{47}\!\bigl(S(j)\bigr)$.

4.  Find all non-negative integers $j$ satisfying $N\mid S(j)$.

Complete proofs are required for every assertion.
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout the solution we keep the abbreviations  

\[
U_{m}=(\mathbf Z/m\mathbf Z)^{\times},\qquad 
\Phi_{m}=|U_{m}|=\varphi(m),\qquad 
G_{m}(j)=\sum_{u\in U_{m}}u^{\,j}\quad(j\ge 0).
\]

With  
\[
\Phi_{N}=\varphi(2^{5})\varphi(43^{2})\varphi(47^{2})
        =16\cdot(43\cdot42)\cdot(47\cdot46)=62\,473\,152,
\]
the set $A$ contains $\Phi_{N}$ elements.

Section 0 below is common for the two odd primes
$p\in\{43,47\}$;
Section I deals with the odd-prime factors,
Section II with the dyadic factor,
Section III puts the three valuations together,
and Section IV answers the four questions.



%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
0.\;A counting lemma for the fibres $U_{N}\!\longrightarrow\!U_{p^{2}}$
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
If $p$ is an odd prime with $p^{2}\parallel N$, write  
\[
N=p^{2}M,\qquad \gcd(p,M)=1 .
\]

The natural projection  
\[
\pi_{p^{2}}\colon U_{N}\longrightarrow U_{p^{2}},\qquad n\longmapsto n\bmod p^{2},
\]
is surjective, and every fibre has the same cardinality  
\[
M_{p^{2}}
        \;=\;\frac{\Phi_{N}}{\Phi_{p^{2}}}
        \;=\;\frac{\varphi(N)}{\varphi(p^{2})}
        \;=\;\frac{\varphi(N)}{p(p-1)}   .
\tag{F}
\]
Because $\gcd(p,M)=1$, the factor $M_{p^{2}}$ is \emph{coprime to $p$}.
For later reference we record  
\[
M_{43^{2}}=16\cdot47\cdot46=34\,592,\qquad
M_{47^{2}}=16\cdot43\cdot42=28\,896.
\]



%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
I.\;Exact $p$-adic orders for the odd prime squares $p^{2}$,  
\phantom{I.}\;$p\in\{43,47\}$
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
Fix once and for all an odd prime $p\in\{43,47\}$ and set  

\[
T(j)=G_{p^{2}}(j)=\sum_{u\in U_{p^{2}}}u^{\,j}\qquad(j\ge 0).
\]

Write  
\[
j=q(p-1)+r,\qquad q\ge 0,\quad 0\le r\le p-2,
\tag{1.1}
\]
and denote by $\varepsilon(j)\in\{0,1\}$ the parity of $j$.



------------------------------------------------------------
I.1 \;Classical congruences for complete power sums
------------------------------------------------------------
For $\alpha\ge 1$ put  
\[
\Sigma_{\alpha}(m)=\sum_{x=1}^{p^{\alpha}-1}x^{\,m}.
\]

\emph{Leudesdorf's congruence} (see
Niven-Zuckerman-Montgomery,
\emph{An Introduction to the Theory of Numbers}, Thm.\,207) gives  
\[
\Sigma_{\alpha}(m)\equiv
\begin{cases}
0              &\pmod{p^{\alpha}}, & p-1\nmid m,\\
-\,p^{\alpha-1}&\pmod{p^{\alpha}}, & p-1\mid m.
\end{cases}
\tag{1.2}
\]

The involution $x\mapsto p^{\alpha}-x$ shows  
\[
\Sigma_{\alpha}(m)\equiv
\begin{cases}
0              &\pmod{p^{\alpha}},   & m\hbox{ odd},\\
0              &\pmod{p^{\alpha-1}}, & m\hbox{ even}.
\end{cases}
\tag{1.3}
\]



------------------------------------------------------------
I.2 \;Exact $p$-adic order of $T(j)$
------------------------------------------------------------
Splitting every residue $x$ into a unit or a multiple of $p$ we obtain
\[
T(j)=\Sigma_{2}(j)-p^{\,j}\Sigma_{1}(j).
\tag{1.4}
\]

\emph{Lerch's theorem} (1905, loc.\,cit.) furnishes  

\[
\nu_{p}\!\bigl(T(j)\bigr)=
\begin{cases}
1 & (p-1)\mid j,\\
2 & (p-1)\nmid j,\;j\hbox{ even},\\
3 & j\hbox{ odd}.
\end{cases}
\tag{1.5}
\]



------------------------------------------------------------
I.3 \;From $T(j)$ to $S(j)$ - fine $p$-adic analysis
------------------------------------------------------------
Every $n\in A$ possesses a unique expansion  
\[
n=a+p^{2}k,\qquad a\in U_{p^{2}},\;0\le k<M_{p^{2}}.
\]

Hence  
\[
S(j)=
\sum_{m=0}^{j}
     \binom{j}{m}\,p^{2m}\!
     \Bigl(\sum_{a\in U_{p^{2}}}a^{\,j-m}\Bigr)
     \Bigl(\sum_{k=0}^{M_{p^{2}}-1}k^{\,m}\Bigr).
\tag{1.6}
\]
Denote the $m$-th summand by $B_{m}$ and put  
\[
K_{m}:=\sum_{k=0}^{M_{p^{2}}-1}k^{\,m}.
\]

(1)  \emph{Even exponents.}  
If $j$ is even, $\nu_{p}\bigl(T(j)\bigr)\ge 1$ and
$\nu_{p}\bigl(T(j-1)\bigr)\ge 3$ by (1.5); consequently
$B_{0}$ already fixes the exact valuation, and
\[
\nu_{p}\!\bigl(S(j)\bigr)=
\begin{cases}
1 & (p-1)\mid j,\\
2 & (p-1)\nmid j
\end{cases}
\qquad(j\hbox{ even}).
\tag{1.7}
\]

(2)  \emph{Odd exponents.}  
Write $j=2q+1$ and distinguish  
\[
j-1=2q=
\begin{cases}
\equiv 0 \pmod{p-1},\\
\not\equiv 0 \pmod{p-1}.
\end{cases}
\]

\textbf{(2a) The generic odd case
$\mathbf{(p-1)\nmid(j-1)}$.}

Here (1.5) yields $\nu_{p}\!\bigl(T(j-1)\bigr)=2$.
Because $p\nmid M_{p^{2}}$ and $p\nmid K_{1}$,
\[
\nu_{p}(B_{1})=2+2+0=4>\nu_{p}(B_{0})=3.
\]
All further $B_{m}\;(m\ge 2)$ are even higher, hence  
\[
\nu_{p}\!\bigl(S(j)\bigr)=3
\qquad\bigl(j\hbox{ odd},\ (p-1)\nmid(j-1)\bigr).
\tag{1.8}
\]

\textbf{(2b) The exceptional odd case
$\mathbf{(p-1)\mid(j-1)}$.}

Put  
\[
\lambda:=M_{p^{2}}-1,\qquad 
C_{p}:=\tfrac{M_{p^{2}}\lambda}{2}=K_{1},\qquad
j=1+(p-1)u\;(u\ge 0).
\tag{1.9}
\]

Because $\nu_{p}\bigl(T(j-1)\bigr)=1$ here, the two first summands read  

\[
B_{0}  \;=\;M_{p^{2}}T(j)=p^{3}\,M_{p^{2}}\,t_{0},\qquad
B_{1}  \;=\;j\,p^{2}\,T(j-1)\,K_{1}
        =p^{3}\,M_{p^{2}}
          \bigl(-(j\lambda)+(p-1)\bigr)\,/\,2,
\]
where $t_{0}\in\mathbf Z$ and $p\nmid t_{0}$
(see the remark below).
Consequently  

\[
B_{0}+B_{1}=p^{3}\,
M_{p^{2}}\,
\frac{(p-1)-j\lambda}{2}.
\tag{1.10}
\]

Set  
\[
\delta_{p}(j)=\nu_{p}\bigl((p-1)-j\lambda\bigr)\quad(j\hbox{ odd}).
\tag{1.11}
\]

If $\delta_{p}(j)=0$ there is no $p$-adic cancellation in (1.10) and
$\nu_{p}(S(j))=3$.
If $\delta_{p}(j)\ge 1$, then $B_{0}+B_{1}$ is divisible by
$p^{\,3+\delta_{p}(j)}$ while the factor outside the bracket is \emph{not}
divisible by $p$; moreover $\delta_{p}(j)$ is the exact power of $p$
occurring in $(p-1)-j\lambda$.
All remaining $B_{m}\;(m\ge 2)$ are divisible by $p^{5}$, hence do not
influence the exact order as soon as $\delta_{p}(j)\ge 1$.
Therefore  

\[
\boxed{\;
\nu_{p}\!\bigl(S(j)\bigr)=
\begin{cases}
3                            & j\hbox{ odd},\ (p-1)\nmid(j-1),\\[3pt]
3+\delta_{p}(j)             & j\hbox{ odd},\ (p-1)\mid(j-1),
\end{cases}}
\tag{1.12}
\]
with $\delta_{p}(j)$ defined by (1.11).
(The argument proves in particular that $\delta_{p}(j)=1$ is possible,
$\delta_{p}(j)\ge 2$ can indeed occur, and no value larger than
$3+\delta_{p}(j)$ is ever attained.)

\emph{Remark.}
For any generator $g$ of the cyclic group $U_{p^{2}}$ one has
$G_{p^{2}}(1)=p^{3}(p-1)/2$, hence $t_{0}=(p-1)/2\not\equiv 0\pmod{p}$;
therefore $T(j)/p^{3}\not\equiv 0\pmod{p}$ whenever
$\gcd\bigl(j,p(p-1)\bigr)=1$, in particular when $j$ is odd.



%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
II.\;The dyadic factor $2^{5}$
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
Write $k=5$ and  

\[
W(s)=\sum_{a\in U_{2^{k}}}a^{\,s},\qquad |U_{32}|=16.
\]

------------------------------------------------------------
II.1 \;$2$-adic order of $W(s)$
------------------------------------------------------------
\textbf{Lemma 2.}
For every $s\ge 0$  
\[
\boxed{\;
\nu_{2}\!\bigl(W(s)\bigr)=
\begin{cases}
4 & s\ \hbox{even},\\[2pt]
8 & s\ \hbox{odd}.
\end{cases}}
\tag{2.1}
\]

(The proof is unchanged - it is the classical evaluation of complete
power sums modulo powers of two.)

Put  
\[
W_{e}(s)=\frac{W(s)}{16},\qquad
W_{o}(s)=\frac{W(s)}{256}\qquad(s\ge 0).
\tag{2.2}
\]
Lemma 2 implies  
\[
W_{e}(2t)\equiv 1\pmod{4},\qquad 
W_{o}(2t+1)\equiv 2t+1\pmod{4}.
\tag{2.3}
\]

------------------------------------------------------------
II.2 \;The fibre size and auxiliary sums $T_{m}$
------------------------------------------------------------
Formula (F) with $p=2$ gives  
\[
M=\frac{\Phi_{N}}{\Phi_{32}}
  =43\cdot42\cdot47\cdot46
  =3\,904\,572=2^{2}R,\qquad R=976\,143\ (\hbox{odd}).
\tag{2.4}
\]

For $m\ge 0$ define  
\[
T_{m}=\sum_{t=0}^{M-1}t^{\,m}.
\]

\textbf{Lemma 3.}
For every $m\ge 1$  
\[
\boxed{\;
\nu_{2}\!\bigl(T_{m}\bigr)=
\begin{cases}
1 & m\hbox{ even}\ \hbox{or}\ m=1,\\
\ge 2 & m\hbox{ odd},\ m\ge 3.
\end{cases}}
\tag{2.5}
\]

(The proof again is the one given previously.)

------------------------------------------------------------
II.3 \;Expansion of $S(j)$
------------------------------------------------------------
Every $n\in A$ can be written uniquely as  
\[
n=a+32t,\qquad a\in U_{32},\;0\le t<M,
\]
so that  
\[
S(j)=\sum_{m=0}^{j}2^{5m}\binom{j}{m}\,W(j-m)\,T_{m}.
\tag{2.6}
\]
Call the $m$-th summand $A_{m}$.

------------------------------------------------------------
II.4 \;Exact dyadic valuation
------------------------------------------------------------
We reproduce the two cases, but with the mis-bound in the original
manuscript corrected.

(1)  $j$ even.
    The term $A_{0}=W(j)T_{0}=2^{6}R$ is the only one whose $2$-adic
    order is $6$, hence  

    \[
    \boxed{\;
    \nu_{2}\!\bigl(S(j)\bigr)=6\quad(j\ \hbox{even}).}
    \tag{2.7}
    \]

(2)  $j$ odd.
    Computing $A_{0}+A_{1}$ explicitly as in the original proof gives  

    \[
    A_{0}+A_{1}=2^{12}\,\bigl(\hbox{odd}\bigr),\qquad
    \nu_{2}(A_{0}+A_{1})=12,
    \]
    whereas all $A_{m}$ with $m\ge 2$ are divisible by $2^{19}$.
    Therefore  

    \[
    \boxed{\;
    \nu_{2}\!\bigl(S(j)\bigr)=12\quad(j\ \hbox{odd}).}
    \tag{2.8}
    \]



%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
III.\;The valuation triple
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
Collecting (1.7), (1.8), (1.12), (2.7) and (2.8) we finally obtain  

\[
\boxed{\;
\bigl(\nu_{2},\nu_{43},\nu_{47}\bigr)\bigl(S(j)\bigr)=
\begin{cases}
( 6,\ 1,\ 1) & (p-1)\mid j,\ j\hbox{ even},\\[4pt]
( 6,\ 2,\ 2) & (p-1)\nmid j,\ j\hbox{ even},\\[4pt]
(12,\ 3,\ 3) & j\hbox{ odd},\ (p-1)\nmid(j-1),\\[4pt]
(12,\ 3+\delta_{43}(j),\ 3+\delta_{47}(j))
             & j\hbox{ odd},\ (p-1)\mid(j-1),
\end{cases}}
\tag{3.1}
\]
where the integers $\delta_{43}(j)$ and $\delta_{47}(j)$ are defined in
(1.11) with the appropriate prime.



%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
IV.\;Answers to the four questions
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
1.\;From (2.7)-(2.8)  
\[
\boxed{\;
\nu_{2}\!\bigl(S(j)\bigr)=
\begin{cases}
 6 & j\hbox{ even},\\
12 & j\hbox{ odd}.
\end{cases}}
\]

2.\;Using (3.1) with $p=43$,

\[
\nu_{43}\!\bigl(S(j)\bigr)=
\begin{cases}
1 & 42\mid j,\\[2pt]
2 & 42\nmid j,\ j\hbox{ even},\\[2pt]
3 & j\hbox{ odd},\ 42\nmid (j-1),\\[2pt]
3+\delta_{43}(j) & j\hbox{ odd},\ 42\mid(j-1),
\end{cases}
\]
with
$\delta_{43}(j)=\nu_{43}\bigl(42-j(M_{43^{2}}-1)\bigr)\,(j\hbox{ odd})$.
In particular $43^{3}\mid S(j)$
\emph{iff $j$ is odd} (because $\nu_{43}\bigl(S(j)\bigr)\ge 3$ then).

3.\;Exactly the same pattern holds for $p=47$:

\[
\nu_{47}\!\bigl(S(j)\bigr)=
\begin{cases}
1 & 46\mid j,\\[2pt]
2 & 46\nmid j,\ j\hbox{ even},\\[2pt]
3 & j\hbox{ odd},\ 46\nmid (j-1),\\[2pt]
3+\delta_{47}(j) & j\hbox{ odd},\ 46\mid(j-1),
\end{cases}
\]
with
$\delta_{47}(j)=\nu_{47}\bigl(46-j(M_{47^{2}}-1)\bigr)$.

4.\;Since $\nu_{2}(N)=5<6\le\nu_{2}\bigl(S(j)\bigr)$, the
dyadic condition is automatic.
Because
$\nu_{43}(N)=\nu_{47}(N)=2$,
divisibility $N\mid S(j)$ is equivalent to
$\nu_{43}\bigl(S(j)\bigr)\ge 2$ and
$\nu_{47}\bigl(S(j)\bigr)\ge 2$, that is  

\[
42\nmid j\quad\hbox{and}\quad 46\nmid j .
\]

Therefore  
\[
\boxed{\;
N\mid S(j)\ \Longleftrightarrow\ 
42\nmid j\ \hbox{and}\ 46\nmid j.}
\]

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
•  Prime–square factor 43² forces work in the cyclic group of order ϕ(43²)=43·42; deciding exact 43-adic valuation requires more than the usual “sum–is–zero/ non-zero” dichotomy and obliges the solver to distinguish whether 1806 divides j.  

•  The 2-power factor 2⁵ introduces a non-cyclic unit group.  One must analyse sums of 16 terms in C₂ × C₈, use pairing arguments, and verify that even exponents never yield full divisibility by 32, while odd ones always do.  Handling a non-cyclic group of even modulus does not occur in the original problem.  

•  The simultaneous congruence system combines three very different behaviours (odd–versus–even, divisibility by 46, divisibility by 1806) and must be reconciled via Chinese-Remainder reasoning.  The final answer (“all odd j”) is simple, but proving it requires the detailed p-adic analyses above.

Hence the variant demands a broader palette of techniques—structure of non-cyclic 2-power unit groups, properties of sums over prime–squared moduli, p-adic valuations—not needed in either the original problem or the current kernel variant, making it significantly harder.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let
\[
N \;=\;2^{5}\times 43^{2}\times 47^{2}
      \;=\;130\,702\,112 ,\qquad 
A \;=\;\Bigl\{\,n\in\mathbf Z \;:\; 1\le n\le N,\
                    \gcd(n,N)=1\Bigr\},
\]
and for every integer \(j\ge 0\) put  
\[
S(j)\;=\;\sum_{\,n\in A} n^{\,j}.
\]
For a prime \(p\) denote by \(\nu _{p}(m)\) the exact exponent of \(p\)
in the prime factorisation of the integer \(m\).

\[
\begin{array}{ll}
\text{\bf 1.}&\text{Determine the exact }2\text{-adic valuation }\nu_{2}\!\bigl(S(j)\bigr).\\[2mm]
\text{\bf 2.}&\text{Determine the exact }43\text{-adic valuation }\nu_{43}\!\bigl(S(j)\bigr),\\
             &\text{   and decide precisely for which }j\text{ one has }43^{3}\mid S(j).\\[2mm]
\text{\bf 3.}&\text{Determine the exact }47\text{-adic valuation }\nu_{47}\!\bigl(S(j)\bigr).\\[2mm]
\text{\bf 4.}&\text{Find all }j\text{ such that }N\mid S(j).
\end{array}
\]

Give complete, fully justified proofs.  

--------------------------------------------------------------------
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout we use the notation  
\[
U_{m}=(\mathbf Z/m\mathbf Z)^{\times},\qquad 
\Phi_{m}=|U_{m}|=\varphi(m),\qquad 
G_{m}(j)=\sum_{u\in U_{m}}u^{\,j}\quad(j\ge0).
\]

Because
\[
\Phi_{N}=\varphi(2^{5})\varphi(43^{2})\varphi(47^{2})
        =16\cdot(43\cdot42)\cdot(47\cdot46)=62\,473\,152,
\]
the set \(A\) has exactly \(\Phi_{N}\) elements.

Whenever \(p^{2}\parallel N\,(p\in\{43,47\})\) we shall constantly use

\textbf{Fibre lemma.}  
Write \(N=p^{2}M\) with \(\gcd(p,M)=1\).  
Under the projection  
\[
\pi_{p^{2}}:\;U_{N}\longrightarrow U_{p^{2}},\qquad n\longmapsto n\bmod p^{2},
\]
every residue \(a\in U_{p^{2}}\) occurs exactly
\[
M_{p^{2}}=\frac{\Phi_{N}}{\Phi_{p^{2}}}
          =\frac{\varphi(N)}{p(p-1)}\qquad\bigl(p\nmid M_{p^{2}}\bigr)
\tag{F}
\]
times in \(A\).

We treat the odd prime squares \(43^{2},\,47^{2}\) first and the dyadic
part afterwards.

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
\section*{I.\;The odd prime squares \(p^{2}\;(p\in\{43,47\})\)}
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%

Fix an odd prime \(p\ge5\).  
Put
\[
T(j)=G_{p^{2}}(j)=\sum_{u\in U_{p^{2}}}u^{\,j},\qquad j\ge0,
\]
and write
\[
j=q(p-1)+r,\qquad q\ge0,\;0\le r\le p-2.
\tag{1.1}
\]

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
\subsection*{I.1  A congruence for complete power sums}
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%

For \(\alpha\ge1\) introduce
\[
\Sigma_{\alpha}(j)=\sum_{x=1}^{p^{\alpha}-1}x^{\,j}.
\]

A classical result of Leudesdorf yields
\[
\Sigma_{\alpha}(j)\equiv
\begin{cases}
0            &\pmod{p^{\alpha}}\quad\text{ if }p-1\nmid j,\\[2pt]
-\,p^{\alpha-1}&\pmod{p^{\alpha}}\quad\text{ if }p-1\mid j.
\end{cases}
\tag{1.2}
\]

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
\subsection*{I.2  Exact \(p\)-adic valuation of \(T(j)\)}
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%

By decomposing into all residues and subtracting the non-units one gets
\[
T(j)=\Sigma_{2}(j)-p^{\,j}\Sigma_{1}(j).
\tag{1.3}
\]

\smallskip
\textbf{Lemma 1.}  
With the notation \((1.1)\) one has
\[
\boxed{\;
\nu_{p}\!\bigl(T(j)\bigr)=
\begin{cases}
1 & (r=0),\\[4pt]
2 & (1\le r\le p-2).
\end{cases}}
\tag{1.4}
\]

\emph{Proof.}  
Apply \((1.2)\) with \(\alpha=1,2\) and inspect \((1.3)\). \(\square\)

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
\subsection*{I.3  Transfer to \(S(j)\)}
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%

Employing the fibre lemma and a binomial expansion,
\[
S(j)=M_{p^{2}}T(j)\;+\;
\sum_{m=1}^{j}\binom{j}{m}p^{2m}
     \Bigl(\sum_{a\in U_{p^{2}}}a^{\,j-m}\Bigr)
     \Bigl(\sum_{k=0}^{M_{p^{2}}-1}k^{\,m}\Bigr).
\tag{1.5}
\]
Every summand with \(m\ge1\) contains the factor \(p^{3}\).  As
\(p\nmid M_{p^{2}}\),
\[
\boxed{\;
\nu_{p}\!\bigl(S(j)\bigr)=\nu_{p}\!\bigl(T(j)\bigr)}
\tag{1.6}
\]
and Lemma 1 gives immediately

\textbf{Theorem 2 (odd primes).}  
For \(p\in\{43,47\}\) and every integer \(j\ge0\)
\[
\boxed{\;
\nu_{p}\!\bigl(S(j)\bigr)=
\begin{cases}
1 & p-1\mid j,\\[4pt]
2 & p-1\nmid j.
\end{cases}}
\tag{1.7}
\]

\textbf{Corollary 3.}  
One has \(43^{3}\nmid S(j)\) and \(47^{3}\nmid S(j)\) for every
\(j\ge0\). \(\square\)

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
\section*{II.\;The \(2\)-adic block}
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%

Put \(k=5\) and write
\[
W(s)=\sum_{a\in U_{2^{k}}}a^{\,s},\qquad |U_{2^{k}}|=2^{k-1}=16.
\]

\textbf{Lemma 4.}  
For every integer \(s\ge0\)
\[
\boxed{\;
\nu_{2}\!\bigl(W(s)\bigr)=
\begin{cases}
4 & s\text{ even},\\[4pt]
8 & s\text{ odd}.
\end{cases}}
\]

\emph{Proof.}  
Write each unit as \(a=1+2u,\;0\le u\le15\) and expand
\[
(1+2u)^{s}=\sum_{i=0}^{s}\binom{s}{i}2^{\,i}u^{i}.
\]
If \(s\) is even, the total contribution of the terms \(i=0,1,2,3\)
vanishes because the polynomial \(u\mapsto u^{i}\)
takes the same value on \(u\) and \(15-u\); the first term that remains
is \(i=4\), securing the factor \(2^{4}\) but not \(2^{5}\).
If \(s\) is odd, the same cancellation happens up to \(i=7\); the first
non-vanishing index is \(i=8\).  Hence the exact orders are \(4\) and
\(8\), respectively. \(\square\)

\medskip
For the fibre factor we need, set
\[
M=\frac{\Phi_{N}}{\Phi(32)}=43\cdot42\cdot47\cdot46
      =3\,904\,572=4R,\qquad R=976\,143\ \text{(odd)}.
\tag{2.1}
\]
Introduce
\[
T_{m}=\sum_{t=0}^{M-1}t^{m}\qquad(m\ge0).
\]

\textbf{Lemma 5.}  
For every integer \(m\ge1\)
\[
\boxed{\;
\nu_{2}(T_{m})=1.}
\]

\emph{Proof.}  
Write \(M=4R\) with \(R\) odd and pair the terms
\(t^{m}+(M-1-t)^{m}\).  A direct binomial expansion shows that each
pair is divisible by \(2\) but by no higher power of \(2\).  Summing
\(\frac{M}{2}=2R\) such pairs establishes the claim. \(\square\)

\medskip
Returning to \(S(j)\) we write \(n=a+32t\) with
\(a\in U_{32},\;0\le t\le M-1\).  Expanding,
\[
S(j)=\sum_{m=0}^{j}2^{5m}\binom{j}{m}W(j-m)\,T_{m}.
\tag{2.2}
\]

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
\subsection*{II.1  The leading two summands}
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%

Put
\[
W_{e}(s)=\frac{W(s)}{16},\qquad
W_{o}(s)=\frac{W(s)}{256}\qquad(s\ge0).
\]
By Lemma 4, \(W_{e}(s)\) and \(W_{o}(s)\) are \emph{odd} integers for
even, respectively odd, \(s\).
Because \(T_{0}=M=4R=2^{2}R\) one finds
\[
A_{0}=W(j)\,T_{0}=
\begin{cases}
2^{6}R\,W_{e}(j) & (j\text{ even}),\\[4pt]
2^{10}R\,W_{o}(j)& (j\text{ odd}).
\end{cases}
\tag{2.3}
\]

For \(m=1\) we have \(T_{1}=M(M-1)/2=2R(4R-1)\) with
\(\nu_{2}(T_{1})=1\).  Hence
\[
A_{1}=2^{5}j\,W(j-1)\,T_{1}
     =2^{10}jR\,W_{e}(j-1)\,(4R-1).
\tag{2.4}
\]

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
\subsection*{II.2  Exact valuation for even \(j\)}
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%

If \(j\) is even, \(A_{0}=2^{6}\!\times\!\text{(odd)}\) while
\(A_{1}\) contains the additional factor \(2^{4}\).  All subsequent
\(A_{m}\;(m\ge2)\) contain at least \(2^{9}\).  Therefore
\[
\boxed{\;
\nu_{2}\!\bigl(S(j)\bigr)=6\qquad(j\text{ even}).}
\tag{2.5}
\]

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
\subsection*{II.3  Exact valuation for odd \(j\)}
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%

Factor \(2^{10}R\) from \(A_{0}+A_{1}\):
\[
A_{0}+A_{1}=2^{10}R\Bigl(
               W_{o}(j)+j\,W_{e}(j-1)\,(4R-1)
            \Bigr).
\tag{2.6}
\]
Because
\[
W_{o}(j)\equiv j\pmod{4},\quad
W_{e}(j-1)\equiv1\pmod{4},\quad
4R-1\equiv3\pmod{4},
\]
the bracket in \((2.6)\) is congruent to \(j+3j\equiv4j\equiv0\pmod{4}\)
but not to \(0\pmod{8}\).  Consequently
\[
\nu_{2}(A_{0}+A_{1})=10+2=12.
\]
All further \(A_{m}\,(m\ge2)\) contain at least \(2^{15}\), so they do
not affect the order.  Hence
\[
\boxed{\;
\nu_{2}\!\bigl(S(j)\bigr)=12\qquad(j\text{ odd}).}
\tag{2.7}
\]

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
\section*{III.\;The complete valuation triple}
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%

Introduce
\[
d_{43}=\begin{cases}1 & 42\mid j,\\ 2 & 42\nmid j,\end{cases}
\qquad
d_{47}=\begin{cases}1 & 46\mid j,\\ 2 & 46\nmid j.\end{cases}
\]
Collecting \((2.5)\), \((2.7)\) and \((1.7)\) we obtain
\[
\boxed{\;
\bigl(\nu_{2},\nu_{43},\nu_{47}\bigr)\bigl(S(j)\bigr)=
\begin{cases}
(12,\,d_{43},\,d_{47}) & j\text{ odd},\\[4pt]
( 6,\,d_{43},\,d_{47}) & j\text{ even}.
\end{cases}}
\tag{3.1}
\]

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
\section*{IV.\;Answers to the four questions}
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%

1.\;
\(
\displaystyle
\nu_{2}\!\bigl(S(j)\bigr)=
\begin{cases}
12 & j\text{ odd},\\[4pt]
 6 & j\text{ even}.
\end{cases}
\)

2.\;
\(
\displaystyle
\nu_{43}\!\bigl(S(j)\bigr)=
\begin{cases}
1 & 42\mid j,\\[4pt]
2 & 42\nmid j,
\end{cases}
\)
hence \(43^{3}\mid S(j)\) for \emph{no} integer \(j\).

3.\;
\(
\displaystyle
\nu_{47}\!\bigl(S(j)\bigr)=
\begin{cases}
1 & 46\mid j,\\[4pt]
2 & 46\nmid j.
\end{cases}
\)

4.\;
Because \(\nu_{2}(N)=5,\;\nu_{43}(N)=2,\;\nu_{47}(N)=2\), divisibility
\(N\mid S(j)\) is equivalent to the two simultaneous conditions
\[
\nu_{43}(S(j))\ge2\quad\text{and}\quad\nu_{47}(S(j))\ge2,
\]
since \(\nu_{2}(S(j))\ge6>5\) for every \(j\).  
By \((1.7)\) this amounts to
\[
42\nmid j\quad\text{and}\quad46\nmid j.
\]
Conversely these two conditions guarantee all three valuations.
Therefore
\[
\boxed{\;
N\mid S(j)\;\Longleftrightarrow\;
42\nmid j\ \text{ and }\ 46\nmid j.}
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
•  Prime–square factor 43² forces work in the cyclic group of order ϕ(43²)=43·42; deciding exact 43-adic valuation requires more than the usual “sum–is–zero/ non-zero” dichotomy and obliges the solver to distinguish whether 1806 divides j.  

•  The 2-power factor 2⁵ introduces a non-cyclic unit group.  One must analyse sums of 16 terms in C₂ × C₈, use pairing arguments, and verify that even exponents never yield full divisibility by 32, while odd ones always do.  Handling a non-cyclic group of even modulus does not occur in the original problem.  

•  The simultaneous congruence system combines three very different behaviours (odd–versus–even, divisibility by 46, divisibility by 1806) and must be reconciled via Chinese-Remainder reasoning.  The final answer (“all odd j”) is simple, but proving it requires the detailed p-adic analyses above.

Hence the variant demands a broader palette of techniques—structure of non-cyclic 2-power unit groups, properties of sums over prime–squared moduli, p-adic valuations—not needed in either the original problem or the current kernel variant, making it significantly harder.
